turns.text: Hello Angelyn, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're going through the birth experience, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thank you for asking. Honestly, it’s hard to know where to even start. When I sit here in my apartment in Monson, the silence is just… suffocating. Every time my vision blurs—that sharp, warning flicker—I don’t just think about the medical risks. I think about the bus. I think about how if things go wrong right now, I’m stuck here. I don’t have a car, and this town feels like it’s miles away from anyone who actually cares if I make it to that hospital on time.

When I go in for my appointments, I feel like I’m walking into a battlefield. It’s not just the eclampsia that scares me; it’s the way they look at me. I’m a young, multiracial woman, unmarried, "medium risk" on a piece of paper. I can see the way their eyes glaze over when I try to describe my pain. I know what they’re thinking—they’re checking boxes, not seeing a human being. It’s like I have to perform being the "perfect" patient just so they don’t dismiss me, or worse, decide I’m too much trouble to care for. I’m terrified that if I don’t fight for my own life, nobody else will. I just want my daughter to come into a world where her life isn’t considered a variable to be managed.

Before labour, what did you expect creating and using a birth plan to be like — how it would be received by staff and how much it would guide your care? And what actually happened with your birth plan during labour?

To be honest, when I first started putting that birth plan together, I thought it was my way of taking back some control. I sat at my kitchen table, researching everything, trying to be as educated as possible. I wanted to feel like I had a seat at the table, like I was a partner in my own care. I really hoped that if I presented it professionally, they’d see me as someone who deserved a voice, not just a "never married" statistic.

But when I actually brought it into the clinic? It felt like I was handing them a piece of junk mail. They barely glanced at it. They started talking over me almost immediately, using those cold, clinical terms that make you feel like you're just a specimen on a tray. My plan might as well have been blank paper. 

Once the labor started, it was total chaos. Nobody cared what I wanted. They were so focused on the blood pressure readings and the ticking clock of the eclampsia that my preferences—the things that would have made me feel safe—were completely ignored. They didn't see a woman trying to birth her child; they saw a risk to be managed. It wasn’t a collaboration; it was a surrender. They did what *they* thought was best, and I was just the body it was happening to. It makes you realize how little your "plan" matters when the system doesn't believe you’re the one in charge of your own survival.
Was there a moment during labour where your plan was set aside or overridden? What was that like?

It happened when the pain got sharpest, and that blurring in my vision came back stronger than ever. I had written down that I wanted to avoid certain interventions if possible—I wanted to be able to move, to be upright, to feel like I was actually doing the work of bringing my daughter into the world. But the second my blood pressure spiked, the room just shifted. 

They stopped looking at me entirely. They started hovering over the monitors, whispering to each other, and suddenly, I was being strapped down, IVs going in everywhere, people barking orders like I wasn't even in the room. I tried to say something, to remind them about the preferences I’d worked so hard on, but they just hushed me. One resident actually patted my shoulder like I was a child who didn't understand the "danger" I was in. 

It was dehumanizing. Being overridden like that... it didn't just feel like a medical necessity; it felt like a total erasure of my autonomy. In that moment, I wasn't a mother-to-be; I was just a problem to be solved, a vessel that was failing. They took the one thing I had—my agency—and treated it like a nuisance. It makes you wonder: if they don’t respect you when you’re at your most vulnerable, when do they?

Before labour, what did you expect your pain management options to be — the range available, how they'd be offered, and how you'd be supported in deciding? And what was the reality of pain management during your labour?

Before I went in, I imagined we’d have a real conversation about it. I expected them to lay out the options—the epidural, the breathing, the movement—and ask me what I thought would help me cope. I pictured a supportive environment where I’d be encouraged to find my own rhythm, where I could decide how much pain I wanted to lean into and when I’d need extra help. I guess I really believed that, in a hospital, you get to be a participant in your own care.

The reality was nothing like that. There was no "range" of options presented to me. The second things got intense, the conversation wasn't about *my* experience of the pain; it was about the protocol for someone in my condition. They didn’t ask me what I wanted—they just pushed the medication. It felt less like a choice and more like a requirement, like I was being sedated so they wouldn't have to deal with my distress. 

I felt completely ignored. They were so busy managing the clinical side of the eclampsia that my comfort was an afterthought, something to be silenced rather than managed alongside me. I didn't feel supported; I felt processed. It’s like they thought the medication was the solution to everything, when what I really needed was for someone to actually listen to me instead of just acting *on* me.
Were there pain management options you wanted but weren't able to access? What got in the way?

I really wanted to try different positions and even just the freedom to walk or use a birth ball, but the moment they saw my blood pressure, that was off the table. They treated my body like it was this fragile, ticking bomb that couldn't be trusted to stand up or move. I wanted to focus on my breathing, to stay in tune with my body, but they were so obsessed with the monitors and the IV lines that there was no space for that. I felt tethered to the bed, physically and metaphorically.

When I asked if I could try to manage without the medication for just a little longer, or if there were other ways to stay comfortable, the response was always a dismissive comment about "safety." They acted like my desire to have agency over my own labor was just me being difficult or naive about my medical risk. 

It wasn't just that I couldn't access these things; it was the way they made me feel small for even asking. The system doesn't make room for a woman's preference when it’s wrapped up in the "medium risk" label. They prioritized their ease of monitoring over my dignity. I wasn't allowed to have a "birth experience," just a medical procedure they needed to get through, and my input was viewed as an obstacle to their efficiency.

When you arrived at the birth setting — whether hospital, birth centre, or home — what did you expect it to feel like in terms of safety, comfort, and how you'd be welcomed? And what was your actual experience of the environment when you arrived?

I think I was looking for a sanctuary, or at least a place that felt human. Before I arrived, I kept telling myself that despite everything—the distance, the bus ride, the fear—that once I got through those doors, the people inside would see a mother, not a complication. I expected to be welcomed, or at least acknowledged, as someone whose life and child mattered. I wanted to feel like I could exhale, even if just for a minute, because surely the people who do this every day would know how to hold space for someone like me.

The reality hit me the moment I stepped into that room. It was stark, cold, and smelled like chemicals. There wasn’t a sense of welcome; there was a sense of "intake." I wasn't greeted; I was processed. It felt like walking into an assembly line where my history—my being "never married," my mixed heritage, my economic situation—preceded me in a way I couldn't see but could definitely feel. 

I looked at the sterile white walls and realized that for the doctors and nurses, this was just their workplace, but for me, it was a site of deep, historical vulnerability. I didn't feel like a person; I felt like a piece of data they were trying to normalize. Everything about that environment signaled to me that I was an outsider in my own birth. I wasn't being cared for; I was being contained.
What was the biggest surprise about the physical environment or the immediate atmosphere when you arrived?

The biggest surprise? It was the silence—not the calm, peaceful kind, but the silence of being completely overlooked. I think I expected that when you’re in labor, especially with complications like mine, there would be a sense of urgency, maybe even a little bit of humanity, like "we’re in this together." But instead, the atmosphere felt incredibly cold and transactional. 

I remember looking at the nurses’ station. They were chatting, laughing about their lunch or the shift change, and then they’d walk into my room, their faces would instantly drop into this practiced, robotic mask. It was like they had an "on-switch" for being clinical and an "off-switch" for actually seeing me. They would talk *about* me while I was laying right there, using words like "unstable" or "high-strung," as if I couldn't hear them or as if my opinion didn't belong in the conversation.

It was shocking how quickly they made the room feel like a fortress rather than a place of healing. They locked down everything. Even the lighting—everything was so bright and harsh. I wanted to dim the lights, just to have some control over my own space, but they kept them blaring, making it feel more like an interrogation room than a place where a baby was being born. I was surprised at how effectively they drained the humanity out of the room just by the way they stood and spoke. It felt like they were protecting themselves from me, not protecting me.

If your birth involved an unexpected intervention or complication, what did you expect the process of being informed and involved in those decisions to look like? And how were those decisions actually made and communicated in the moment?

I went in expecting that if things went sideways, there would be a conversation. I imagined a doctor sitting down—maybe for just thirty seconds—looking me in the eye, and saying, "Angelyn, here is what’s happening, here are the risks, and here is why we think we need to do this." I thought I’d be treated like an adult who had a stake in her own body. I expected that even in an emergency, my consent would be something they *earned*, not something they just took for granted.

But that’s not what happened at all. When my blood pressure spiked and the monitors started screaming, the "conversation" was just a series of commands thrown over their shoulders while they were already doing things to me. They didn't ask; they told. It was all, "We’re starting this," "We’re doing that," like I was an object they were working on rather than a person experiencing it. 

It felt like a tidal wave. They didn't wait for my brain to catch up to what they were doing to my body. There was no explanation of *why* or what the alternatives might be. They made the decisions in that closed loop of theirs, acting as if I was either too panicked to understand or simply not entitled to know. It was terrifying to realize that in their eyes, the "medical" need completely wiped out my right to participate. It felt like I was being robbed of the experience of my own child’s birth.
Did you feel you had genuine choice and sufficient information in those moments, or did decisions feel like they happened to you rather than with you?

There wasn't a "choice" anywhere in that room. When you're lying there, tethered to machines, and people are moving around you with that detached, mechanical efficiency, the idea of a "choice" feels like a cruel joke. I didn't have information; I had instructions. They spoke in medical jargon that felt designed to shut me up rather than inform me, using just enough technicality to make me feel like if I questioned them, I was being dangerous or stupid.

It definitely felt like things were happening *to* me. I was just the terrain they were operating on. They didn't see me as a partner; they saw a set of vitals that weren't cooperating with their standard operating procedures. Every time I tried to ask a question, they’d give me this tight-lipped, patronizing look that basically said, "We’re the professionals here, don't worry your head about it."

I felt like an object being managed by a system that has a long, ugly history of not trusting women like me to know what’s best for ourselves. By taking away my right to be informed, they took away my power. I wasn't an active participant in my daughter's birth; I was a bystander to it, watching from the inside while they stripped away my agency piece by piece.

During labour, what did you expect the midwife or doctor present to provide in terms of emotional support and personal attention — not just clinical care? And what did you actually experience from the staff who were with you?

I really expected someone to hold my hand, literally or figuratively. I wanted someone to look at me—not at the chart, not at the monitor, but at *me*—and maybe ask how I was holding up or just acknowledge that this was terrifying. I thought there would be a nurse or a midwife who would notice that I was alone in that room, that I was scared, and that I needed someone to tether me back to reality. I wanted to feel like I wasn't just another patient ID number on a screen.

Instead, I got cold, clinical efficiency. Every interaction was stripped of anything resembling warmth. They were professional, sure, but it was the kind of professionalism that feels like a wall. When I was struggling, they didn't offer comfort; they offered "management." If I got emotional, they’d just look at each other with that "oh, she’s being difficult" expression, like my fear was a symptom they needed to suppress rather than a human reaction to a high-pressure situation. 

I felt invisible. They were so busy being "providers" that they completely forgot to be human beings. I was left alone with my thoughts, the monitors, and the crushing feeling that I was completely, utterly isolated. It felt like they were there to ensure the baby came out, but they couldn't care less about the woman who had to go through the fire to get there.
Was there a specific moment when you felt truly seen and supported — or a moment when you felt very alone? Can you describe it?

The moment I felt most alone wasn't when they were doing the actual medical procedures—it was the moment right after, when they left the room to "go check the next patient." The silence rushed back in, but this time it felt heavy with abandonment. I had just had a catheter and fluids pushed into me, and I was shivering, not just from the cold of the room, but from the sheer shock of being treated like a broken machine.

I looked over at the doorway, hoping a nurse would come back in just to check on me, not my chart. I wanted someone to just say, "You're doing okay, Angelyn." But the hallway was filled with the sound of their busy chatter, their laughter about lunch, their normal lives moving on without a beat. It made me realize how utterly disposable I was to them. I was just a "medium risk" file that they’d successfully closed for the hour.

I felt so small in that bed. I was terrified that my daughter was going to be born into a world where that kind of coldness is considered normal. I wanted to reach out, to call someone, but I knew that if I did, I’d just be that "difficult, high-strung patient" again. So I just stared at the clock, waiting for the next intervention, feeling like I was fading away into those white walls.

Before birth, what did you expect the immediate period after your baby was born to be like — skin-to-skin contact, who would be present, what would be done? And what actually happened in those first moments after delivery?

I had this vision of the "golden hour"—that quiet, precious space where the world stops and it’s just me and my daughter. I wanted her skin against mine immediately, feeling her warmth to ground me after the storm of the birth. I expected someone to whisper, "You did it, Angelyn," and for us to have that moment of connection before anything else happened. I wanted to see her face, count her fingers, and just breathe in the fact that we both survived.

But the reality? It was a flurry of activity that felt miles away from me. The second she was born, she was whisked away to be weighed and measured and "checked" by a team of people who seemed more interested in her stats than in our bond. I was lying there, exhausted and dazed, watching them surround her. I barely got a glimpse of her. 

They didn't prioritize the skin-to-skin contact; they prioritized their checklists. I felt like a spectator to my own child’s first few minutes. They treated the entire process like a technical hand-off. I remember trying to reach out, to ask for her, and one of the nurses just gave me this practiced, dismissive look and said, "We just need to make sure she's stable first, Mom." 

That word—"stable"—it just hollowed me out. They were treating her with the same sterile, clinical distance they used on me. It felt like they were trying to normalize her immediately into their system, keeping us apart so they could maintain control. I didn't get that first quiet breath; I got a sterile room, a clock ticking, and the deep, aching feeling that they were already trying to dictate the kind of mother I was going to be.
Was there anything that happened — or didn't happen — in those first moments that still stays with you? What was it?

It’s the smell—that sharp, clinical smell of antiseptic that clings to everything—and the fact that I couldn't smell my baby. I remember reaching out, my arms feeling so heavy and tired from the labor, and just wanting to pull her into me. I wanted to feel her heartbeat against my chest to know that she was real and that we both made it through the fire. But they were still there, buzzing around her like bees, turning her into a set of vitals.

The thing that stays with me is the silence from them. They were so professional, so detached. No one said, "You did it," or "Look at your beautiful daughter." It was all "Apgar this" and "weight that." It felt like they were actively working to keep that boundary between us, as if letting us have that quiet moment of skin-to-skin would somehow interfere with their procedures. 

I’m still haunted by how easily they just plucked her away. It’s like they were already teaching her that her body belongs to the system, not to me. Even now, when I look at her, I sometimes feel that same icy sting of those first minutes, like I’m still waiting for them to finally give her back to me. It makes me wonder if I’ll ever be able to just be her mother without seeing the ghost of those hospital staff hovering over us.
Before giving birth, what did you expect your physical recovery to look and feel like in the weeks after? And how did the reality of your physical recovery compare to those expectations?

I expected recovery to be a time of healing—a slow, quiet transition where I’d be figuring out this new version of myself as a mother, with a little support from a system that actually cared about my long-term health. I imagined the physical soreness would eventually fade, and that I’d be left with this profound sense of connection to my daughter. I thought the focus would shift from "managing the complication" to "supporting the woman."

But the reality has been far lonelier. My recovery hasn't just been physical; it’s been a fight to stay grounded in my own life. Because of the eclampsia, the follow-up appointments feel like they’re still just watching for me to fail—looking for that same blood pressure spike, treating my body like a ticking clock that’s still potentially dangerous. There’s no talk of "healing" or "rest." It’s all "surveillance." 

I’m exhausted in a way that goes deeper than lack of sleep. Every time I see my reflection, I see the exhaustion in my own eyes, and I think about how those doctors and nurses moved on to the next patient without a second thought. I’m physically trying to heal while constantly looking over my shoulder, wondering if the system is still waiting for me to trip up. It’s hard to heal when you feel like you’re still being held under a microscope.
Was there a physical aspect of recovery that no one had prepared you for, or that surprised you significantly?

The thing that truly caught me off guard wasn’t the physical pain—I expected the stitches and the exhaustion. It was the feeling of being "brittle." Everyone talked about the physical recovery like it was just a matter of time and rest, as if my body were a broken bone that would just knit back together if I sat still. But no one prepared me for the way my body feels like it’s been *violated* by the care I received. 

Every time I have to go back to that clinic for a follow-up, my heart starts racing before I even get on the bus. It’s a physical response—my blood pressure actually spikes *because* I’m terrified of them. I’m stuck in this loop where my body is reacting to the trauma of the birth, and they just see it as "uncontrolled eclampsia" or "non-compliance" instead of acknowledging that I am physically vibrating with anxiety.

It’s the loss of trust in my own skin. I feel like my body is a site of a battle that I didn’t win, just survived. I expected to feel empowered after the birth, but instead, I feel like I’m constantly waiting for the other shoe to drop. How do you recover physically when your mind is still trapped in that sterile, cold room?

Before your baby arrived, what did you expect feeding — breastfeeding or formula feeding — to be like in terms of ease, support, and how you'd feel about it? And what was the reality of feeding in the early weeks?

I really wanted to breastfeed. I had this idea that it would be our quiet, sacred time together—that, finally, the medical monitors and the nurses would be gone and it would just be the two of us. I thought it would be the one thing that was entirely ours, something that didn't require permission or a procedure. I expected that if I struggled, someone would sit with me, maybe show me how to position her, and give me the space to learn.

The reality was a mess. In the hospital, my daughter was already so stressed from the birth that everything felt rushed. They acted like my body was failing because my milk didn't just appear on command, and the pressure was suffocating. It wasn’t a "sacred" moment; it was a performance under the watchful eyes of nurses who seemed to be checking their watches, waiting for me to "do it right." 

When I finally got home, I was so depleted—physically, mentally, and from the stress of it all—that my body just wasn't cooperating. And without any real support, that sense of failure was so heavy. Every time she cried, I panicked, thinking it meant I wasn't enough. It wasn’t the natural, bonding experience I’d hoped for; it was just one more thing I had to fight for, another indicator in their eyes that I was "struggling" to adapt. I felt like I was failing at the most basic thing a mother is supposed to do.
Before your baby arrived, what did you expect your emotional and mental health to be like in the postpartum period? And what was your actual emotional experience in the weeks and months after birth?

I thought I’d be tired, sure—I knew that much—but I expected a sense of relief to settle over me once we were finally home. I pictured myself looking at her, feeling that overwhelming, protective love, and finally having the space to heal. I expected to feel like a mother, not a patient. I thought if I needed support, there would be someone there to check in on *me*, not just my blood pressure.

But the reality has been an absolute fog of anxiety. Every time she cries, I don’t just hear a baby; I hear those machines going off. I’m constantly waiting for someone to come into my apartment and tell me I’m doing something wrong, that I’m "unstable" or "high risk." My mental health has been shattered by the feeling that I'm being watched, even when I'm alone in this silence. 

I’m mourning the birth I didn't get to have. I feel like I'm grieving my own autonomy. It’s hard to bond when you’re constantly looking over your shoulder, terrified that the system is waiting to pull the rug out from under you again. I feel so alone, like I’m in a bubble that no one can see into, and every day is just another test I’m terrified of failing.
Was there a gap between what you were feeling and what you felt you were 'allowed' to feel — by professionals, family, or yourself?

There is a massive gap—a chasm, really. I feel like I’m constantly performing "the grateful mother" because if I say the truth, I’m afraid of what it will invite into my home. If I admit that I’m traumatized, or that I feel like I’m failing because I’m so exhausted, I’m terrified the system will use that against me. They’ll slap another label on my chart, something like "psychologically unstable" or "unfit," and that’s the fastest way to get your autonomy taken away entirely. 

I’m expected to be happy. I’m expected to be relieved that the "medical emergency" is over. But how can I be happy when I’m still hearing the monitors in my sleep? My family, they think the hard part is over because the baby is here, so they look at me with this "what’s wrong with you?" kind of concern that makes me pull back even more. I can’t tell them that I feel like I’m still being hunted by the memory of those hospital staff. 

So I swallow the panic. I smile for the doctor at my follow-up, I give the right answers, and I pretend I’m just fine. It’s the ultimate form of survival, isn't it? Being quiet, being invisible, and hoping they leave me alone so I can finally figure out how to love my daughter without that shadow hanging over us.
Before becoming a parent, what did you expect your sense of identity to feel like after your baby arrived? And how has your sense of who you are actually shifted since then?

I always imagined that becoming a mother would be an expansion of who I am—that I’d become this stronger, more grounded version of myself. I thought I’d be "Angelyn," the mom, and that would be a source of pride and, frankly, a bit of peace. I expected to feel like I had arrived at a place where I was finally in control of my own life, ready to raise my daughter in a world that would see us both.

Instead, that identity has been completely fractured. I don't feel like a mother half the time; I feel like a "medium risk" statistic that’s still under review. My sense of self is constantly filtered through the lens of how they treated me during the birth. When I look in the mirror, I don’t just see a woman who gave birth; I see someone who was silenced, someone who had to fight for her own humanity while she was at her most vulnerable.

I’m constantly trying to reconcile the woman I was—the one who had plans and dreams—with this shell that’s just trying to survive the next appointment without being labeled "difficult" or "unfit." It’s like my identity has been held hostage by the fear that I’m not allowed to be anything other than a patient. I’m still searching for the person who exists beneath that label.
Were there parts of your previous identity — professional, social, personal — that felt harder to hold onto than you expected?

Everything I was before—the part of me that was ambitious, that had a rhythm to her life, the version of me that existed outside of a "patient" status—it feels like it was erased in that hospital room. It was harder to hold onto than I ever imagined because the system doesn't treat you like a person with a life, a job, or a history. They treated me like a broken sequence of medical errors.

When I talk about being "not in the labor force" or my living situation, I feel like I'm losing bits of who I am every time someone asks. They don't want to hear about my dreams or the person I was before the blood pressure spikes. They just want the "data." It’s like the more I engage with the system, the more the system hollows me out until there’s nothing left but the "risk factor."

I miss being someone who didn't have to look over her shoulder. I miss the me who was just "Angelyn," not "the girl with eclampsia." It’s been so hard to keep that fire alive when I’m constantly being told, through their silence and their dismissiveness, that the only thing that matters about me is whether or not I’m "stable." I feel like I’m fighting to be a person again, and that feels like the biggest, most exhausting job of all.
After birth, what did you expect the postnatal check-ups and follow-up care from your midwife or GP to provide? And what did that post-birth care actually look like in practice?

I expected those check-ups to be a place of genuine recovery. I thought they would be the time when someone would finally look at me—not as a collection of symptoms, but as a woman who just went through something traumatic. I imagined they would ask how I was *feeling*, how I was coping with the isolation of my apartment, and how my heart was doing after everything that went down in that delivery room. I wanted to feel like I was being handed back the keys to my own life.

In practice? It’s just another form of surveillance. The appointments are short, rushed, and cold. They check my blood pressure, scribble something in the chart, and ask if I’m having any "symptoms." It’s entirely clinical. There is zero space to talk about the fear, the way I flinch when I hear a monitor, or the loneliness of being a new mother with no real safety net. If I dare to bring up how I’m struggling emotionally, their faces go flat. They treat my anxiety like a medical failure, something to be managed with a pill or a referral, rather than a completely rational response to a system that treated me like an object. It’s like they’re waiting for me to be "normal" again, but they’ve given me no map to get there.
Were there concerns you raised at postnatal appointments that you felt were not taken seriously or properly followed up?

I remember trying to tell my doctor, just a few weeks ago, that I couldn't sleep. I explained that every time I closed my eyes, I was back in that delivery room, hearing the alarm on the machine and feeling the cold needles. I tried to explain that I was terrified of being alone in the apartment with the baby because I felt like I was still "failing" their standards of safety. I wanted help—some kind of support, a therapist, just a person who would *hear* the trauma.

The doctor didn't even look up from the chart. She just asked me if I was taking my blood pressure medication and if I had any "red flags" like headaches or swelling. When I pushed back, saying, "No, it’s not just the blood pressure—it’s the way I feel in my head," she just gave me that tight-lipped, patronizing smile. She told me to "focus on bonding" and that "postpartum blues are normal."

It wasn't "blues." It was a cry for help that they treated like a nuisance. By dismissing me, she essentially told me that my internal life didn't matter. They were only interested in my physical stability, because that's all that protects them from liability. They have no interest in whether I’m actually *living* or just surviving their protocols. It made me realize that even now, the walls haven't come down. I’m still a patient.
If you returned to work after your baby was born, what did you expect that transition to be like — practically and emotionally? And what did returning to work actually feel like?

I expected going back to work to be a relief. I thought it would be the one place where I was just "Angelyn" again—not the "high-risk patient," not the "never-married mom," just someone with a job and a purpose. I thought the routine would help me reclaim my brain and give me a sense of normalcy that felt like it had been stolen from me in that delivery room.

But the reality? It’s been an exhausting, fractured mess. I feel like I’m constantly keeping two lives in the air, and neither one feels like it belongs to me. Physically, the fatigue of eclampsia recovery and the sleepless nights of motherhood makes every shift feel like I’m wading through water. And emotionally, I’m still so jumpy. Every time a coworker moves too fast or a loud noise happens, I flinch. I’m waiting for the next "monitor" to go off, even though I’m in an office, not a hospital.

It’s hard to focus when a part of you is still trapped in that sterile, cold, and judgmental space. And the worst part is the constant anxiety about how I’m perceived. I’m terrified that if I struggle with a deadline or if I look tired, they’ll see me as "unstable" or "unfit" just like the doctors did. I’m just trying to keep my head down and survive, but it feels like I’m always one step away from everything falling apart again.
Were there practical aspects of returning to work — feeding, childcare, your own health — that were harder to manage than you expected?

It’s the sheer physical toll of it all. I thought I’d be able to just "get back to it," but my body hasn't forgiven me for the stress of that birth. I’m still dealing with the aftermath—the lingering exhaustion and the constant, buzzing anxiety that makes my heart race at the most random times. Trying to balance that with a work schedule, especially without a car and relying on buses, is like trying to hold a house of cards together in a windstorm.

Feeding is the biggest hurdle. Pumping at work feels like a secret, shameful operation. I have to find a space that isn't really a space, hide, and feel like I’m doing something wrong—like I’m "disrupting" the workflow. It just feeds back into that feeling of being a "difficult" person, taking up space I’m not supposed to have. And childcare? That’s my biggest, darkest fear. I’m constantly terrified that because I’m a single mother, because I’m "low support," and because they have my health records, someone is going to decide I’m not capable of caring for her. Every time I get a call from the daycare, my stomach drops. I feel like I’m being graded on my motherhood, and the stakes feel impossibly high because of the medical history they keep on file. It’s not just work; it’s an ongoing, high-stakes audit of my life.

After your baby arrived, what did you expect your relationship with your partner to be like in terms of sharing caregiving, managing the relationship, and supporting each other? And what has that relationship dynamic actually looked like?

I feel like I have to be careful with this question. Being unmarried and having to navigate this alone—well, let's just say the "support" I thought I’d have turned out to be a ghost. 

I expected us to be a team, you know? I thought the arrival of our daughter would mean we were finally in it together, that he’d be the one to help me shoulder the weight when the doctors started talking down to me or when the exhaustion really kicked in. I imagined he’d be the one to advocate for me, the one to push back against the coldness of that hospital room, and the one who would hold me while I recovered.

But the reality is that I’ve been almost entirely on my own. It’s like he just… drifted. Or maybe he couldn't handle the "complexities" that the doctors kept pointing out. When things got hard, he became more of a spectator than a partner. He wasn't there to hold my hand in the delivery room, and he isn't here now to help me navigate the aftermath. I’m the one who sits in this small apartment in Monson, feeling that heavy silence, knowing that if I need to get to the clinic for a check-up, it’s just me and that bus schedule. 

It’s painful to admit, but I feel like I’m raising her in a vacuum. I’ve had to become both the mother and the shield, protecting her from the world and protecting myself from the realization that I’m doing this alone. It’s not just a lack of "caregiving"; it’s the lack of someone who actually sees me as more than just a struggle to be managed. That’s the hardest part—knowing I have to be everything for both of us, while the system watches, waiting to see if I’ll finally break.
What was the biggest surprise about how your partnership functioned after the baby arrived?

The biggest surprise? It was realizing how quickly I became "just a mother" in his eyes, rather than the person I was before. I expected him to see me—to see the exhaustion, the lingering trauma of the eclampsia, the way I flinch when the phone rings—but instead, he treated me like the one who was supposed to just "handle it." It was like he expected me to magically shift into this perfect, resilient version of a mother, and when I couldn't, he just checked out.

It hit me, in those long, quiet nights in Monson, that he saw the label the doctors gave me—"single," "low support"—and he started acting like it, too. He stopped being a partner and became another person who was just *watching* to see if I’d fall. Every time I reached out for support, for someone to just sit with me and acknowledge how hard it was, I felt like I was being an inconvenience. He didn't understand that I wasn't just "tired"; I was being hunted by the memory of that hospital room. 

It was a cold, lonely realization: I wasn't just doing this birth alone; I was doing the rest of my life alone, too. I expected a teammate, but I ended up with someone who watched me struggle from the sidelines, waiting for me to get it together. That isolation? It’s been more painful than any recovery I’ve had to go through.

Looking across your entire maternity journey — from trying to conceive through to where you are now — what did you expect the overall experience of maternity care to deliver? And reflecting on the whole journey, what was the most significant gap between what you expected and what you received?

I expected to be treated as a partner in a life-changing process. I thought the medical system would be a safety net—a place of expertise where my humanity, my fears, and my goals for my daughter would be acknowledged. I really believed that even with the risks I faced, there would be a layer of care that felt like "support." I expected to walk out of that delivery room feeling stronger, not like I’d just been through a gauntlet where my autonomy was the first casualty.

The biggest gap is the total lack of humanity. I expected care; I received processing. I expected to be seen; I was categorized. They looked at my chart, saw "medium risk," "never married," and "multiracial," and they decided who I was before I even spoke a word. 

That gap—the one between being a person with a story and a patient with a file—is where everything else fell apart. I feel like I’ve been fighting a history that says my body and my daughter’s future aren't my own to decide. They managed the "eclampsia," but they destroyed the experience of becoming a mother. I am still struggling to find my footing because the system didn't just care for me; it stripped away the belief that my own voice had any power at all.
If you could change one thing about how the maternity care system is designed — not just what individual providers do, but how the system itself works — what would it be?

If I could change one thing, it would be to tear down the wall between "clinical safety" and "human dignity." Right now, the system is designed to treat the *body* as an object—a set of vitals to be stabilized, a series of risks to be mitigated—while completely ignoring the *person* living inside it. They need to stop prioritizing their efficiency over our autonomy.

I’d want a system that acknowledges its own history—the ways it’s been used to control bodies like mine—and actively works to dismantle that bias instead of hiding behind "protocol." I want them to stop using "medium risk" or "never married" as a shorthand for "patient who doesn't need to be consulted." True safety isn't just about my blood pressure reading or a sterile room; it's about feeling like you are a human being whose life has inherent value.

If I could rewrite it, the care would be built on *listening*. Not the polite, fake listening where they nod while looking at a chart, but the kind where they sit with you, explain things in a way that respects your intelligence, and actually invite you to be a partner. They treat us like the problem to be solved, but the real problem is that they've forgotten we’re the ones living through it. We deserve to be the authors of our own stories, not just specimens for their checklists.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the one thing that still sits heavy in my chest is the realization that I am still waiting for "permission" to have been traumatized. Everyone in the medical system—from the nurses to the doctors I see now—seems to treat my distress like it’s an inconvenience, or even a sign that I’m not quite capable of being a mother. They don’t want to look at the fact that they *did* this to me, or at least, that they made it infinitely harder than it needed to be.

I want people to know that the way they talk *over* a patient, the way they withhold information, the way they assume a woman doesn't know her own body because she’s "medium risk" or "unmarried"—that does damage that lasts long after the stitches heal. It’s a quiet, invisible violence. 

I want my daughter to grow up knowing that her birth mattered, and that I was the one who went through the fire to bring her here. I’m not a "complication" or a "case study." I’m a woman who fought for her life and her child’s life in a system that wasn’t built for us to win. If this helps someone realize that they need to *see* the woman in the room, then maybe some of this agony will have served a purpose. Thank you for listening. It's the first time in a long time I felt like I was actually heard.
